title: On Software Estimation — Longform Conversation
author: Host Persona and Guest Persona
text:
HOST: Welcome back to the show. Today I'm talking with a guest who has spent two decades trying to figure out why software estimates are always wrong, and what, if anything, can be done about it. Thanks for coming on.

GUEST: Happy to be here. And I want to push back gently on the framing already. It is not that estimates are always wrong. Estimates are often wrong, and they are systematically biased, but the more interesting question is why people keep asking for them in organizations where everyone knows they are unreliable. That is the puzzle.

HOST: That is a good correction. So let us start there. Why do organizations keep asking for estimates?

GUEST: Because an estimate is the currency of cross-team coordination. If I am building a feature and your team is building the thing that depends on my feature, you need some way to plan your work. You cannot say I will start when you finish. You need a date. That date is the estimate. So the estimate is doing a job that has very little to do with prediction and a lot to do with coordination.

HOST: But then the estimate is not really an estimate. It is a commitment.

GUEST: Exactly. And the confusion between those two things is the source of most of the pain in software projects. An estimate, in the technical sense, is a probabilistic forecast based on prior evidence. A commitment is a contract, implicit or explicit, about when something will be delivered. Organizations ask for estimates and treat them as commitments, and engineers give estimates that they know internally are more like commitments, and then everybody is surprised when the commitment slips, because in their heart of hearts they knew it was always an estimate with a wide confidence interval.

HOST: Let us walk through a concrete case. I am a software engineer. My manager says how long will this take. What is actually happening inside my head?

GUEST: You are doing several things at once. You are trying to map the work to things you have done before. You are trying to anticipate the unknowns. And you are trying to read the social signal. Is this a question about capacity planning, or is this a question about whether we should do the project at all, or is this a question about whether we can promise a customer a date, or is this a question about whether my manager thinks I am being ambitious enough. All of those framings change the answer.

HOST: Does the manager know they are sending a social signal?

GUEST: Sometimes. Often not. This is one of the reasons the estimation conversation is broken. The manager thinks they are asking a technical question. The engineer is reading it as a social and political question. And even when both sides know this is happening, the language does not have good tools to separate out the pieces.

HOST: So what would a better language look like?

GUEST: The simplest step, and this is borrowed from NASA and from construction and from a lot of older engineering disciplines, is to give the estimate as a range with a confidence level. Not here is my number. Instead, here is my eightieth-percentile range. I am eighty percent confident the work will take between two and six weeks. Then the question becomes which decisions do we need to make, and can we make those decisions with a range this wide. If we cannot, we need to spend some time narrowing the range, or we need to make a different decision.

HOST: Do you find engineers are good at giving eightieth-percentile ranges?

GUEST: Terrible at it. There is a classic result from the calibration literature, which is that most people, including most experts, when asked to give a ninety percent confidence interval, give a range that is actually their forty or fifty percent confidence interval. They are wildly overconfident. The good news is that calibration is trainable. If you give people feedback on their ranges over several weeks or months, their calibration gets much better. The bad news is that nobody does this in practice, because the feedback loops are too long.

HOST: Feedback loops being too long is a theme that comes up a lot in your work. Say more about that.

GUEST: A software project that takes three months to deliver generates exactly one data point about the accuracy of the initial estimate. If that estimate turned out to be wrong, you learn that it was wrong, but you do not usually learn why, because the project had dozens of intermediate decisions and the reasons for the slippage are hard to disentangle. If you want engineers to get better at estimation, you need to give them many more reps with much faster feedback. That is one of the underrated advantages of shipping small. Shipping a feature in two weeks lets you calibrate against a two-week estimate. Shipping a feature in a quarter lets you calibrate against a quarterly estimate, and quarters are much rarer than two-week intervals in a career.

HOST: So shipping small is not just about risk. It is about learning.

GUEST: It is mostly about learning, I would argue. The risk reduction is a side effect of being able to inspect your work more frequently.

HOST: Let us talk about cone-of-uncertainty graphs. You are skeptical.

GUEST: I am skeptical of how they are used. The cone of uncertainty is a real phenomenon. At the start of a project, you know very little, so your confidence interval is wide. As the project proceeds and you learn about the problem, the interval narrows. That is real. What happens in practice, though, is that people draw the cone as a promise. They draw a cone that is wide at the start and a neat point at the end, and they plan around the point. The reality is that the cone narrows if and only if you are actively working to reduce uncertainty. If you are not, it stays wide. A project can be six weeks in and have exactly as much uncertainty as it had on day one, because nobody has been doing the work of resolving the open questions.

HOST: How do you know whether a team is doing the work of resolving uncertainty?

GUEST: You ask what questions they have answered this week that they did not know the answer to last week. If they cannot name any, the cone has not narrowed. You do this ritually. Every week. You are not asking about progress in the usual sense. You are asking about uncertainty reduction. This is often a different question from the one teams are used to being asked.

HOST: I want to get into the role of architecture in estimation. Some teams claim they cannot estimate until they have designed the system. Other teams claim the design emerges from the estimation process. Which is right?

GUEST: Both, at different points. Early in a project, when the problem is fuzzy, you have to do enough design to know what you are building. Without that, your estimates are fantasies. There is no such thing as a reliable estimate for a system you have not even sketched. On the other hand, once you have sketched the system, you can make the estimation process itself into a design tool. You look at the pieces, you ask which ones you know how to build and which ones you do not, and the pieces you do not know how to build are the real work. The estimation conversation forces the design conversation, because you cannot put numbers on fog.

HOST: Is there a size of project where this breaks down?

GUEST: Yes. The method I have been describing works well up to about a team-quarter of effort, maybe two team-quarters if you are lucky. Beyond that, the uncertainty compounds in ways you cannot chase away with up-front design. For anything longer than a quarter, you have to treat the project as a portfolio of smaller pieces, each of which you can estimate and steer independently. The people who get into real trouble are the ones who try to treat a year-long project as a single estimation problem. That always fails. The project is not one project. It is a dozen. You need to estimate, ship, and learn on each of them in turn.

HOST: Let us talk about the economics of bad estimates. What is the cost of being systematically wrong?

GUEST: There are three costs, and they are different. The first is the direct cost. Your engineers spent longer than planned, so the feature cost more than planned. This is the cost everyone worries about, and it is usually the smallest of the three. The second cost is the opportunity cost. Because you committed to project A and it ran late, you did not do project B, or you did project B with a smaller team, and the value you lost from that is much larger than the direct overrun. The third cost, and this is the biggest one, is the cost to the credibility of the estimation conversation itself. Once an organization internalizes that estimates are unreliable, it stops using them as a tool for coordination, and then the coordination has to happen through escalation and negotiation and political maneuvering, which is extremely expensive. The expense does not show up on any line item. It shows up as friction.

HOST: That last piece is hard to measure.

GUEST: Very hard to measure. But if you walk into a company that has lost trust in its own estimates, you can feel it within a week. Everyone is guarded. Everyone adds buffers to their buffers. Cross-team projects take twice as long as they should because every interface is negotiated as if the other team is trying to deceive you. The culture becomes adversarial.

HOST: What is the recovery path, if a company is already in that state?

GUEST: It is slow. You have to rebuild trust the way you rebuild any trust. You make smaller commitments. You hit them. You do this for six months. Then you make slightly bigger commitments, and you hit those. You keep the scope of what you commit to smaller than you know you can actually deliver. You over-deliver, visibly, on the small stuff. After a year or two of this, the organization remembers what it felt like to trust an estimate. There is no shortcut. People do not start trusting you again because you promised them a better process.

HOST: I want to ask about the role of story points and velocity. Most agile teams use them. You have been public about your skepticism.

GUEST: My skepticism is not of points. Points are fine. My skepticism is of velocity as a planning artifact. Velocity is a measurement of what a team has done. It is a rearward-looking number. When teams use velocity as a forward-looking commitment, they are committing to the thing they were able to do in the past, not the thing they are able to do now. If the work has changed, if the team composition has changed, if the codebase has grown, if the dependencies have shifted, the velocity is stale. Teams get in trouble when they treat velocity as a rate rather than as a retrospective measurement.

HOST: Is there a better alternative?

GUEST: Throughput. Count the number of pieces of work you complete per unit of time. Stop assigning them abstract sizes. If you keep the pieces of work small and roughly similar in size, which is a discipline, throughput becomes a much more honest measurement than velocity. You cannot game it. You cannot inflate it by taking on bigger stories. It just counts what you shipped.

HOST: That requires a discipline around keeping stories small.

GUEST: It does. That discipline is valuable in its own right. If your team cannot keep stories small, you have a different problem than an estimation problem. You have a work-breakdown problem. Solving the breakdown problem solves a lot of other problems downstream.

HOST: We have about ten minutes left. I want to close on the cultural piece. You have written that estimation is a cultural artifact. Expand on that.

GUEST: The way an organization estimates tells you what the organization values. Organizations that demand precise estimates before they will commit to a project are saying that predictability matters more than speed. Organizations that work with rough ranges and adjust as they learn are saying that learning matters more than predictability. Neither of these is wrong in principle. Different problems call for different tradeoffs. But a lot of organizations have not noticed which tradeoff they are making. They demand precision in every meeting, then are surprised when their teams optimize for looking precise rather than for being right.

HOST: How does a team push back on a culture of false precision?

GUEST: Carefully. The pushback has to come with a replacement, not just a critique. You cannot say estimates are bad. The organization will hear that as irresponsibility. You have to say estimates are too coarse a tool for the thing we are trying to do here. We propose to use ranges and to update them weekly. We propose to make smaller commitments and deliver on them. We propose to track throughput instead of velocity. Each of these is a specific, observable change. You offer them as replacements, and you measure the results. If the results are better, the organization learns. If the results are not better, you learn. Either way you are ahead of where you were when you were just generating false-precision estimates and watching them slip.

HOST: What is the one thing you wish every engineer knew about estimation?

GUEST: That the estimate is a conversation, not a number. The number is the summary of the conversation. If the conversation was thorough and the assumptions were clear, the number is informative. If the conversation was rushed and the assumptions were implicit, the number is dangerous. Spend your effort on the conversation, not on the number.

HOST: What is the one thing you wish every manager knew?

GUEST: That asking for an estimate is asking for information, and information has a cost. A ten-minute estimate tells you one thing. A two-hour estimate tells you a different, more reliable thing. A two-day estimate tells you a much more reliable thing. You are choosing how much uncertainty you are willing to live with, and you are paying in engineering hours for the reduction in uncertainty. Treat it that way. Do not ask for a two-day estimate when you can live with the ten-minute answer, and do not ask for a ten-minute answer when the decision you are making is worth a two-day estimate.

HOST: Good place to stop. Where can people find your writing?

GUEST: I keep an occasional newsletter. People can find it through my website, which I will let you link in the show notes.

HOST: We will do that. Thanks again for coming on.

GUEST: Thanks for having me. Good conversation.

HOST: That was my conversation with a software estimation researcher who has been thinking about these questions for twenty years. A few things I want to highlight for listeners. The framing of estimate versus commitment is worth sitting with. A lot of friction in engineering organizations lives in the gap between those two concepts. The suggestion to give ranges with confidence levels is simple and almost never adopted. The observation that the cone of uncertainty only narrows if you are actively working to narrow it is one of those things that sounds obvious the moment you hear it but transforms how you run project check-ins. And the point about estimation as cultural artifact, I think, is underrated. Most estimation debates are really debates about what the organization values and whether the team and the leadership have aligned on those values.

HOST: Before we go, I want to come back to one thread we did not get to fully develop in the main conversation, which is the question of how estimation interacts with hiring and onboarding. You mentioned earlier that feedback loops are too slow. One of the things that makes them slow is that new engineers often spend their first six months doing work that the senior engineers on the team would have estimated very differently. What is your advice for teams with a lot of junior or recently-onboarded members?

GUEST: The answer is probably disappointing. You have to lower the stakes of the estimate. A junior engineer cannot give you a reliable estimate for a complex piece of work. They do not yet have the pattern library. What they can do is give you a reliable estimate for a well-scoped piece of work, especially if it is similar to something they have recently done. So the work has to be scoped smaller, and the pairing with a more experienced engineer has to be part of the scope itself, and the variance on the estimates has to be accepted as part of the cost of training people. A team that is growing fast should have wider ranges on every estimate, full stop, and leadership should know that.

HOST: Is there a point where a team is too junior to do useful estimation at all?

GUEST: Yes. I have seen teams where every estimate was noise, and the right move was to stop asking for them for a quarter while the team caught its breath and learned the codebase. That is a hard thing for leadership to hear. But you cannot out-process your way around a fundamental capability gap. You have to actually close the gap.

HOST: Okay, I lied about closing. One more question. What is the worst estimation advice you see in circulation today?

GUEST: Multiply your estimate by two. Or by pi. Or by some fixed inflation factor. This is a very popular piece of engineering folklore. It is, in my view, actively harmful. The fact that your estimates are biased does not mean they are biased by a constant multiplier. They are biased by specific, identifiable causes, and those causes are different on different projects. Applying a blanket multiplier creates the illusion that you have solved the estimation problem when you have only papered over it. And worse, it trains your organization to distrust the number you give, because they know you doubled it, which means they have to guess what you actually believe. You have introduced a second layer of negotiation. Better to give the honest estimate, give it as a range, be explicit about the assumptions, and let the organization negotiate those assumptions rather than negotiate the multiplier.

HOST: Great answer to close on. Thanks again.

GUEST: My pleasure.

HOST: Thanks as always for listening. If you enjoyed this conversation, the best thing you can do for the show is share it with somebody who would find it useful. Until next time.
